Quick pharmacology refresher for tomorrow's M&M: warfarin's half-life is roughly 36–42h, so steady-state INR changes lag dose adjustments by ~5–7 days. CYP2C9 inhibitors (fluconazole, amiodarone, metronidazole) potentiate effect; rifampin and carbamazepine induce metabolism and reduce INR. Standard reversal: vitamin K 5–10mg PO for non-urgent supratherapeutic INR without bleeding; 4F-PCC 25–50 units/kg plus IV vitamin K for major hemorrhage. Sourced from Lexicomp and the 2023 CHEST guideline.

Quick primer for the team before tomorrow's call: in most US jurisdictions, an NDA's non-solicitation clause is generally enforceable if it's reasonable in scope, geography, and duration — usually 1–2 years. California is the big outlier; Bus. & Prof. Code §16600 voids most post-employment restraints, and the 2024 amendments (AB 1076) require employers to notify former employees that existing non-competes are void. Worth flagging for the multi-state hiring deck.

Quick pharmacology refresher for tomorrow's rounds: sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor with a half-life of about 26 hours and an active metabolite (desmethylsertraline) that hangs around for 62–104 hours. Typical adult starting dose for MDD is 50 mg PO once daily, titrated up to a max of 200 mg/day. Watch for CYP2D6 inhibition at higher doses and the usual SSRI cautions — serotonin syndrome risk with MAOIs (14-day washout), QT prolongation at supratherapeutic levels, and GI upset in the first 1–2 weeks.

Quick pharmacology refresher for tomorrow's M&M: tramadol is a weak mu-opioid agonist plus SNRI activity (NRI > SRI), metabolized by CYP2D6 to O-desmethyltramadol (M1), which carries most of the analgesic punch. Half-life ~6h for parent, ~7-9h for M1. Watch for serotonin syndrome risk when co-administered with SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs, and seizure threshold lowering at doses >400 mg/day or in patients on bupropion. Ultra-rapid 2D6 metabolizers can get disproportionate opioid effect even at standard 50–100 mg q4–6h dosing.

Quick pharmacology refresher for the team before grand rounds: amiodarone has a half-life of roughly 40–55 days due to extensive lipid sequestration, which is why a loading dose (typically 800–1600 mg/day PO for 1–3 weeks, then tapered to 200–400 mg/day maintenance) is required. Watch for QT prolongation, thyroid dysfunction (both hypo- and hyperthyroidism via the iodine moiety), and pulmonary fibrosis on long-term therapy. CYP3A4 and P-gp interactions are clinically significant — notably with warfarin (reduce dose ~30–50%) and digoxin (reduce dose ~50%).
